questions.question: What single rule decides whether a `.ts` file is treated as a **module** versus a **script**?
options: Whether the file contains at least one top-level `import` or `export`
Whether the file's name ends in `.module.ts`
Whether `module` is set to `esnext` in tsconfig
Whether the file contains any top-level `const` or `function` declaration
questions.explanation: A file becomes a module the moment it has a top-level import or export; otherwise it is a script whose top-level declarations land in the global scope. The `module` compiler option controls emit format, not module-vs-script status.

questions.question: You have a `.ts` file that needs file-local scope but has nothing to export. What is the idiomatic way to force it to be treated as a module?
options: Add `export {}` to the file
Add `declare module {}` to the file
Set `isolatedModules: true` in tsconfig
Rename the file with a `.mts` extension
questions.explanation: `export {}` is an empty export that gives the file a top-level export, forcing module scope without exposing any bindings. `.mts` only affects the emitted module format, not the global-vs-module distinction.

questions.question: A default export is, under the hood, just a named export whose name is `default`, so `import D from "./m"` is equivalent to `import { default as D } from "./m"`.
questions.explanation: A default export is simply a named export named `default`, so both import forms bind the exact same export. `import D from "./m"` is sugar for importing the `default` binding under the name `D`.

questions.question: What does `export * from "./a"` re-export?
options: All of `a`'s named exports, but **not** its default export
All of `a`'s exports including its default export
Only `a`'s default export
A single namespace object bundling all of `a`'s exports
questions.explanation: `export * from "./a"` forwards every named export of `a` but never the default. Bundling everything into one namespace property is `export * as ns from "./a"`, and forwarding the default needs `export { default } from "./a"`.

questions.question: What does the expression `import("./m")` evaluate to at runtime?
options: A `Promise` resolving to the module's namespace object
The module's namespace object synchronously
The module's default export directly
A `require`-style synchronous handle to the module
questions.explanation: Dynamic `import()` is an expression returning `Promise<typeof import("./m")>` — a promise of the namespace object, with the default (if any) at `.default`. It is asynchronous, not synchronous.

questions.question: Given `math.ts` has `export default class Circle {}`, what happens at the line marked `(A)`?
questions.code:
const m = await import("./math");
const c = new m(); // (A)
options: Error: the namespace object is not constructable; you must call `new m.default()`
It works: `m` is the `Circle` constructor, so `new m()` builds a `Circle`
Error: dynamic imports cannot be used with `await`
It works but returns the namespace object rather than a `Circle` instance
questions.explanation: `m` is the module namespace object, whose `default` property is `Circle`. The namespace object itself is not constructable, so `new m()` errors; the correct call is `new m.default()`.

questions.question: Why is marking type-only imports (e.g. `import type` or inline `type`) often **required** when using single-file transpilers like Babel, esbuild, or swc (or with `isolatedModules`)?
options: A per-file transpiler cannot tell whether an imported name is a type or a value, so the marker tells it the import is safe to erase
Those transpilers cannot parse `import` statements without an explicit `type` keyword
The marker is needed so the transpiler emits a `require` call instead of an `import`
Without it, the transpiler treats every import as a default import
questions.explanation: A single-file transpiler sees only one file at a time and cannot do whole-program analysis to know if an imported name resolves to a type or a value. The explicit `type` marker makes erasure deterministic per-file.

questions.question: What is emitted for the following source under `"verbatimModuleSyntax": true, "module": "esnext"`?
options: `import { UserStore } from "./model";`, `const s = new UserStore();`, and `let u;` — the second import is erased
Both import statements are kept verbatim because the file uses both modules
Both import statements are erased because each contains a type specifier
`import { type User, UserStore } from "./model";` is kept exactly as written
questions.explanation: The first statement keeps its value import and strips the inline `type User` specifier, emitting `import { UserStore } from "./model";`. The second is entirely type-only and is erased, and the `: User` annotation on `u` is erased.

questions.question: `declare class Foo {}` describes only the *type* of `Foo` and emits no JavaScript, whereas a normal `class Foo {}` both declares the type and emits a runtime constructor.
questions.explanation: `declare` introduces an ambient, type-space-only declaration with no body and no emit; a normal class declaration produces a real runtime constructor in addition to its type.

questions.question: A `declare global { interface Window { analytics?: ... } }` block successfully adds `analytics` to `Window` everywhere in the program. Which two facts make this work?
options: `declare global` is valid because the file is a module, and the addition merges with the lib's `Window` via declaration merging
`Window` is a type alias, so `declare global` can replace its definition wholesale
`declare global` automatically runs in script files, and `Window` is re-created from scratch
The block emits a runtime patch to `window` and TypeScript infers the type from it
questions.explanation: `declare global` is only valid inside a module (a file with imports/exports), and it relies on declaration merging because `Window` is an open interface. It emits nothing at runtime — it is purely a type-space addition.

questions.question: Which compiler option emits the `.d.ts` type declarations **but no JavaScript at all**, typically used when a bundler produces the runtime output?
options: `emitDeclarationOnly: true`
`declaration: true`
`declarationMap: true`
`skipLibCheck: true`
questions.explanation: `emitDeclarationOnly` emits the type declarations only and skips JS emit. `declaration: true` emits both `.d.ts` and `.js`; `declarationMap` adds go-to-definition maps; `skipLibCheck` skips checking `.d.ts` contents.

questions.question: What does `"skipLibCheck": true` actually do, and what is the cost?
options: It skips type-checking the contents of all `.d.ts` files, speeding builds and avoiding declaration conflicts, but leaves those files unvalidated
It skips downloading `@types` packages, reducing install size but losing IntelliSense
It skips emitting `.d.ts` files, making builds faster but breaking library consumers
It skips checking only third-party `.d.ts` files, while still fully checking your own generated declarations
questions.explanation: `skipLibCheck` skips checking the contents of all `.d.ts` files — yours and dependencies' alike — which speeds builds and sidesteps mutually-incompatible declarations, but means errors in declaration files (including your own generated ones) go undetected.

questions.question: Which statements about the `@types`/DefinitelyTyped ecosystem and TS 6.0 are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: `@types/*` packages are dev dependencies — they vanish at runtime and only the types matter
Under TS 6.0 the `types` option defaults to `[]`, so the compiler no longer auto-includes every `@types/*` package under `node_modules/@types`
A package that ships its own bundled `.d.ts` does not need a separate `@types` package
Under TS 6.0, a previously-implicit global like `process` or `describe` can never go missing because globals are always loaded
DefinitelyTyped declarations are auto-published by the original package authors on every release
questions.explanation: `@types` are dev-only, TS 6.0 makes global `@types` opt-in via `types: []`, and self-typed packages need no `@types`. Option 4 is wrong precisely because `types: []` can make previously-implicit globals disappear; option 5 is wrong because DefinitelyTyped is a community-maintained repository, not author-published.

questions.question: Under `"moduleResolution": "nodenext"`, inside an ESM file whose source is `util.ts`, which relative import specifier is correct?
options: `import { helper } from "./util.js"` — you name the emitted runtime file
`import { helper } from "./util"` — extensionless, since TypeScript rewrites it
`import { helper } from "./util.ts"` — name the actual source file
`import { helper } from "./util.mts"` — the TypeScript ESM extension
questions.explanation: `nodenext` mirrors Node's ESM resolver, which requires explicit extensions, and TypeScript does not rewrite specifiers — so you spell the *emitted* `./util.js`. Extensionless errors under nodenext; `.ts` needs `allowImportingTsExtensions` (with `noEmit`).

questions.question: Given the `package.json` below, which import **fails** and why?
options: `import z from "pkg/dist/internal"` fails: an `exports` map blocks any subpath not listed, even if the file exists
`import y from "pkg/sub"` fails: subpath exports require a trailing slash like `"./sub/"`
`import x from "pkg"` fails: the `"."` entry must list a `default` condition to be importable
None fail: an `exports` map only adds entry points and never blocks deep imports
questions.explanation: Once an `exports` map is present it locks down the public surface: only listed subpaths (`.` and `./sub`) resolve, and `pkg/dist/internal` is blocked even though the file physically exists. That encapsulation is the whole point of `exports`.

questions.question: This file is meant to augment `my-lib`'s `Options` interface but does nothing. What is the root cause?
options: The file has no top-level import/export, so it is a script; `declare module "my-lib"` there declares a brand-new ambient module instead of augmenting the existing one
You cannot add members to an interface via `declare module`; only type aliases can be augmented
`Options` must be re-declared with `export interface` inside the block for merging to occur
Augmentation requires `verbatimModuleSyntax` to be enabled to take effect
questions.explanation: In a script-context file, `declare module "my-lib"` declares a *new* ambient module rather than merging into the real one, so the augmentation silently fails. Making the file a module (e.g. add `export {};` or `import "my-lib";`) fixes it. Interfaces are exactly what you *can* augment.

questions.question: Which of the following can module augmentation (`declare module "lib" { ... }` in a module file) legitimately do? (Select all that apply.)
options: Add a new member to an existing interface in the library, merged via declaration merging
Add members to an existing namespace exported by the library
Add a brand-new top-level runtime *value* export to the library
Change the type of an existing member of the library's interface
Augment a type alias the library exports
questions.explanation: Augmentation only *adds* to open types — interface members and namespace members merge in. It cannot add new top-level value exports, cannot change an existing member's type, and cannot augment a (closed) type alias.

questions.question: Which statements about namespaces, ES modules, and TS 6.0 legacy changes are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: ES modules give per-file scope, real loading semantics via `import()`, and tree-shaking that namespaces lack
In TS 6.0 the old `module Foo {}` spelling is an error; you must use the `namespace` keyword
`namespace N { export ... }` creates a value object `N` at runtime
A namespace participates in module resolution the same way an ES module does
Triple-slash directives may appear anywhere in a file as long as they are comments
questions.explanation: Modules win on scope, loading, and tree-shaking; TS 6.0 errors on `module Foo {}`; and a `namespace` emits a runtime value object. Namespaces do *not* participate in module resolution, and triple-slash directives must appear at the very top of the file before any code.
